questions.question: A 70° shear-wave indication appears at 30 mm soundpath on the first leg (sin 70° = 0.940). How far ahead of the exit point does the reflector lie along the surface?
options: 30 mm
questions.explanation: Surface distance = soundpath × sin θ = 30 × 0.940 ≈ 28.2 mm. The 10.3 mm figure is the depth (soundpath × cos 70°), not the surface distance.

questions.question: An angle-beam weld scan must extend from near the weld toe back to one full skip plus the cap width because:
options: The couplant needs that much area to spread
The first leg only covers the lower half of the weld — the second leg, reached by pulling the probe back, covers the upper half
Codes require scanning twice the heat-affected zone
The dead zone hides the root unless the probe starts far away
questions.explanation: With the probe close in, the beam's first leg interrogates the lower weld; as the probe rasters back toward the full-skip line, the rebounding second leg sweeps up through the upper weld and cap region.

questions.question: Which combination describes accepted practice for a manual contact scan?
options: Single passes with no overlap, at any comfortable speed
Overlapping passes (commonly at least 10–15% of the element width) at no more than about 150 mm (6 in.) per second
50% overlap at a minimum of 300 mm per second
Random scanning until an indication appears
questions.explanation: Overlap guarantees no strip of material goes uninspected, and the speed cap ensures the pulser interrogates every location several times — both are standard procedural requirements.

questions.question: A surface-wave examination must respond only to flaws within about 0.6 mm of the surface of a steel part (Rayleigh velocity ≈ 2.98 mm/µs). Which probe frequency fits best?
options: 1 MHz
2.25 MHz
5 MHz
0.5 MHz
questions.explanation: A Rayleigh wave penetrates about one wavelength: λ = 2.98 ÷ 5 ≈ 0.6 mm at 5 MHz. Lower frequencies would reach deeper than the requirement (about 1.3 mm at 2.25 MHz, about 3 mm at 1 MHz).

questions.question: Lamination screening of thin rolled sheet, where one probe position must cover a wide strip quickly, is a classic application of:
options: Plate (Lamb) waves
Surface (Rayleigh) waves
45° shear waves
Straight-beam multiple-echo thickness reading
questions.explanation: Lamb waves fill the whole thickness of a thin section and travel long distances along it, so a discontinuity anywhere in the sheet disturbs the mode — ideal for fast production screening.

questions.question: Compared with through-transmission, the pulse-echo technique offers:
options: Immunity to coupling variations
Reflector depth from echo timing and access from a single side
Better performance in extremely attenuating material
No dependence on flaw orientation
questions.explanation: Pulse-echo reads round-trip time, locating reflectors in depth, and needs only one surface. Its weaknesses are the opposite of through-transmission's strengths: it has a dead zone and depends on the flaw reflecting energy back to the probe.

questions.question: The tandem technique exists primarily to detect:
options: Laminations parallel to the scanning surface
Surface-breaking cracks at the weld toe
Vertical planar flaws (such as sidewall lack of fusion) in the mid-wall of thick sections
Porosity clusters near the back wall
questions.explanation: A vertical face deflects an angled beam away from a single probe. The tandem pair — same angle, same direction, spaced 2(T−d)·tan θ — catches that deflected energy after it rebounds from the back wall.

questions.question: A dual-element probe resolves reflectors almost at the entry surface mainly because:
options: Its elements operate at a higher frequency than single-element probes
The receiving element never hears the transmit pulse's ringing, eliminating the dead zone
The roof angle converts the beam to a surface wave
Its delay lines double the sound velocity
questions.explanation: Separating transmission and reception (with an acoustic barrier between elements) means the receiver is not saturated by transmit ring-down — the cause of the single-element dead zone.

questions.question: Above approximately what diameter do common code practices allow a curved examination surface to be treated as flat?
questions.explanation: At roughly 500 mm (20 in.) diameter and above, the wedge footprint sees an essentially flat surface, so flat calibration blocks and standard plotting are commonly permitted.

questions.question: On 4 in. schedule 40 pipe (ID/OD ≈ 0.89), a circumferential scan is attempted with a 70° probe (sin 70° = 0.940). The beam will:
options: Reach the bore at exactly 70 degrees
Reach the bore at a shallower angle than 70 degrees
Never reach the inside surface — its closest approach stays within the wall
Mode-convert to a longitudinal wave at the bore
questions.explanation: The chord geometry only lets the beam touch the bore if sin θ ≤ ID/OD. Since 0.940 > 0.89, the 70° beam's closest approach (R·sin θ) lies inside the wall and it skims back to the OD — one reason pipe procedures select angle by diameter and wall thickness.
